story:
I rushed back into Minneapolis General's SICU after maternity leave and ran straight into my new boss: Felicia Gainer, gray hair, colder eyes, and a tongue sharp enough to cut steel. Wally Abara, the nurse manager, greeted me warmly and put Felicia in charge of my reorientation. Felicia made it clear she'd rather demote me than teach me. Most of the staff were new grads who tiptoed around her; instead of mentoring, she sat at the desk and sliced people down.

I tried to change the energy. I brought coffee and donuts to report, slipped into rooms to help when Felicia vanished to "appointments," and coached the rookies when she was out of earshot. At home, my mama warned me: people like Felicia were dangerous. She was right.

Midway through week two, I intubated a crashing patient while Felicia was gone. Wally stood close, breath warm at my neck, and said I could not have done better. His flirtation hummed under the praise, and I stepped away. Felicia walked in eating chocolate and accused me of overstepping. After shift, she hustled Wally into his office. I slipped near the door and overheard it all: she had compromising photos of him and a patient's wife; she'd go to HR unless he got rid of me. Wally tried to defend my performance, but when she pushed, he bargained—if I "failed to measure up" in a week, he'd send me to the floor. Ten dollars an hour less. A baby at home. An ex dodging child support. I rocked my daughter that night and felt the big bad wolf breathing at our door.

In the cold garage I stared at a jug of antifreeze, tasted its sweet bite, and shoved it away. The Nightingale Oath burned in my ears. I'm a nurse. I don't harm.

Next morning, I brought cinnamon rolls and pumpkin spice lattes. I'd marked two lids with jack-o'-lantern stickers to keep track of who'd asked for what. When I hesitated at Felicia—diabetic, insulin-dependent—she sneered and took a latte anyway. I tried to swap it for regular coffee. She sipped and toasted me. Then she turned icy and sent me to prep an old man for surgery. He called me Florence Nightingale and squeezed my hand. I hid my own shaking by humming him a lullaby.

Felicia staggered to the bathroom, sweating and vomiting. Wally thought hypoglycemia; the glucometer flashed "HI"—off the scale, beyond the meter's limit. Severe hyperglycemia, likely a crisis fueled by sweets, dehydration, and mismanaged insulin. The intercom blared: Code Blue, SICU. We shocked her again and again. Ribs snapped. I got the IV, placed an airway, pushed meds, pumped her chest: you gotta live, you gotta live. At 11:46, the resident called it. Cardiac arrest from uncontrolled diabetes.

After, Wally and I cleaned the ruined body. Bruises. Vomit. Ripped clothes. Those cold blue eyes staring up at nothing. I felt no victory, just a blank chill. Wally said people might thank me for outlasting her. He muttered there was no reason for an autopsy; the case would read as a medical complication. He also warned me not to blame myself—Felicia knew sugar was poison to her.

As I drew the sheet, I slipped her phone from her pocket. I'd seen her passcode. I intended to turn those photos over to HR and end the blackmail for good.

Wally asked if I'd consider stepping into Felicia's role. His hand circled my wrist. I met his eyes. "Before you go any further, Mr. Abara, I don't do supply rooms." He laughed too loud. I said I appreciated his confidence. He joked about her size elevens. I walked out with her phone and went back to work. Felicia would have expected it.
